title: Biscuits and Gravy
subtitle: American buttermilk drop biscuits with sausage and cream pan gravy
description: Southern biscuits and gravy is a breakfast and brunch staple of the American South: soft, fluffy buttermilk biscuits split open and smothered with a rich, pork-sausage cream gravy seasoned heavily with black pepper. The biscuits are made by the drop method — no rolling or cutting, just scooped and baked — and the gravy comes together in the same pan the sausage was cooked in. The whole dish takes under forty minutes.
sourceNotes: Biscuits and gravy is a staple of Southern American breakfast and diner cooking, with roots in the Appalachian cooking tradition of the nineteenth century. The dish appears in Fannie Farmer's The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896) in variations, and is a defining dish of American Southern comfort food.

recipeTools.notes: Lined with parchment for the drop biscuits.
For the sausage and gravy — wide enough to cook all the sausage in a single layer.

glossaryTerms.definition: A type of American biscuit made by dropping spoonfuls of a wet, shaggy dough onto a baking sheet — no rolling or cutting required. The high-liquid dough produces a more rustic, irregular shape but is faster and more forgiving than rolled biscuits.

ingredients.notes: Or milk with 1 tbsp white wine vinegar — leave 5 minutes to curdle
Or bulk pork sausage from the casing
Or a mix of milk and cream
Coarsely ground — the pepper is essential and generous

steps: Heat the oven to 220 °C fan (240 °C / gas 9). Line a baking sheet with parchment.

Make the biscuit dough: whisk together the flour, baking powder, bicarbonate of soda, and salt. Add the cold butter cubes and rub into the flour with your fingertips until the mixture resembles rough breadcrumbs — leave some pea-sized pieces of butter intact. Pour in the buttermilk and stir until just combined into a shaggy dough. Do not overmix.
Scoop the dough in 8 rough mounds onto the baking sheet, spacing them 3 cm apart. Bake for 13–15 minutes until risen and golden on top.
While the biscuits bake, make the gravy: cook the sausage meat in a large frying pan over a medium-high heat, breaking it up with a spoon, for 6–8 minutes until browned and cooked through.
Scatter the flour over the sausage and stir well — cook for 1 minute. Pour in the milk gradually, stirring constantly, until the mixture is smooth. Bring to a simmer, stirring regularly.

Season generously with black pepper (at least 1 tsp), salt, and cayenne. Cook for 3–4 minutes until the gravy has thickened to a consistency that coats the back of a spoon. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Split the warm biscuits and spoon the gravy over generously. Serve immediately.

content.text: — no rolling, no cutting, no fuss. The keys are cold butter (kept chunky rather than fully worked in) and not overworking the dough once the buttermilk goes in. Overmixing develops the gluten and turns the biscuits tight and dense. Mix until just combined and scoop immediately.

Heat the oven to 220 °C fan. Whisk {{plain-flour}} of flour with the baking powder, bicarbonate of soda, and salt. Rub in {{butter-unsalted}} of cold butter cubes until the mixture resembles rough breadcrumbs with some pea-sized pieces remaining. Stir in {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk until just combined into a shaggy dough. Scoop 8 mounds onto a lined baking sheet and bake for 13–15 minutes until golden.

Cook {{pork-sausage-meat}} of sausage meat in a large frying pan over a medium-high heat, breaking it up, for 6–8 minutes until browned. Scatter over {{plain-flour}} of flour, stir for 1 minute, then pour in {{whole-milk}} of milk gradually, stirring constantly. Simmer for 3–4 minutes until thickened. Season very generously with black pepper, salt, and cayenne. Split the warm biscuits and ladle the gravy over. Serve immediately.
